title: Navigating Global Divergences: Curtain Raiser Speech at the 2023 IMF Annual Meetings

paragraphs.text: Good morning. It is wonderful to be here in Marrakech for these Annual Meetings, the first to be held in Africa in fifty years. I want to begin by expressing our deep condolences to the people of Morocco following the devastating earthquake last month. The resilience and solidarity of the Moroccan people in the face of this tragedy have been truly inspiring, and I commend the government’s determination to proceed with hosting these meetings.

We meet at a moment of significant divergence in the global economy. Divergences in economic performance, divergences in policy space, and divergences in the capacity of countries to respond to the compounding shocks of recent years. Understanding these divergences and finding ways to bridge them is the central challenge for policymakers gathered here this week.

Let me start with the global growth picture. Our latest World Economic Outlook projects global growth at 3.0 percent for 2023 and 2.9 percent for 2024. While this represents a soft landing relative to the fears of a year ago, it is well below the pre-pandemic average of 3.8 percent. More importantly, this headline figure masks enormous variation across countries and regions. The United States has shown surprising resilience, while the euro area and China face significant headwinds.

The fight against inflation has made genuine progress, but it is not yet won. Global headline inflation is expected to decline from 8.7 percent in 2022 to 6.9 percent in 2023 and 5.8 percent in 2024. Core inflation, however, is proving more stubborn. Central banks must remain vigilant and should not ease policy prematurely. History teaches us that the last mile of disinflation is often the most difficult. Declaring victory too early risks a costly resurgence of price pressures.

The divergence in monetary policy transmission across economies is striking. In the United States, the economy has remained remarkably robust despite significant policy tightening, defying expectations of a recession. Strong consumer spending, a resilient labor market, and fiscal support have all contributed. In contrast, the euro area has experienced a sharper growth slowdown, with the manufacturing sector in contraction and credit conditions tightening substantially. China faces a different set of challenges entirely, with a property sector downturn weighing on growth and confidence.

For emerging markets and developing economies as a group, growth is projected at 4.0 percent, which sounds respectable but again masks wide variation. India stands out as a bright spot, with projected growth above 6 percent. But many low-income countries are struggling with high debt burdens, limited fiscal space, and the lingering effects of food and energy price shocks. For these countries, the global tightening of financial conditions has been particularly painful.

Debt vulnerabilities remain a critical concern. Global public debt now exceeds 93 percent of GDP. More than half of low-income countries are either in debt distress or at high risk of it. The Common Framework for Debt Treatments has achieved results, but the process remains too slow. The cases of Zambia, Ghana, and Ethiopia have demonstrated both the potential and the limitations of the current framework. We need faster, more predictable debt resolution mechanisms.

I am particularly worried about the growing number of countries that are being squeezed between high debt service costs and urgent development needs. When a country spends more on interest payments than on education or health, something is fundamentally wrong. This is not just an economic issue. It is a question of social stability and human dignity. The international community must do more to address this challenge, including through concessional financing, debt relief, and innovative financial instruments.

Geoeconomic fragmentation poses a serious threat to global prosperity. We estimate that the costs of a severe fragmentation scenario could amount to as much as 7 percent of global GDP. The proliferation of trade restrictions, the reorientation of supply chains along geopolitical lines, and the potential for a fragmented global financial system would all reduce efficiency, increase costs, and disproportionately harm the most vulnerable countries and populations.

Let me be clear: trade has been one of the most powerful engines of growth and poverty reduction in modern history. The hundreds of millions of people lifted out of poverty in China, in India, in Southeast Asia, and elsewhere were lifted by integration into the global trading system. We must not allow geopolitical tensions to reverse these gains. This does not mean ignoring legitimate concerns about economic security or supply chain resilience. But it does mean finding approaches that address these concerns without dismantling the open trading system that has benefited so many.

The climate transition presents both enormous risks and enormous opportunities. We estimate that achieving net zero by 2050 will require an additional 6 trillion dollars of annual investment by 2030. Much of this investment must flow to emerging markets and developing economies, where the gap between climate finance needs and availability is largest. Carbon pricing remains the most efficient tool for driving emissions reductions, and we are encouraged by the expansion of carbon pricing mechanisms globally, but coverage remains far too limited.

The IMF has adapted its own toolkit to help members navigate these challenges. The Resilience and Sustainability Trust, which provides affordable long-term financing for climate and pandemic preparedness, has already approved 18 arrangements. Our lending capacity has been strengthened by the channeling of Special Drawing Rights from countries with strong external positions to those with greater needs. We have provided over 50 billion dollars in financing to member countries over the past year alone.

The governance of the IMF must reflect the changing realities of the global economy. The 16th General Review of Quotas, which is under discussion, is an opportunity to ensure that the Fund’s governance structure adequately represents the economic weight and voice of all members. Emerging markets and developing economies have increased their share of global GDP significantly over the past two decades, and this should be reflected in their quota shares and voting power.

I want to emphasize the critical importance of international cooperation at this juncture. The challenges we face — climate change, debt distress, pandemic preparedness, the governance of artificial intelligence — are inherently global in nature. They cannot be solved by any country acting alone, and they cannot be solved in a fragmented world. The multilateral system, imperfect as it is, remains our best instrument for collective action.

Building resilience must be a priority for all countries. The frequency and severity of shocks — whether from pandemics, climate events, geopolitical conflicts, or financial disruptions — has increased markedly. Countries need stronger fiscal buffers, more flexible exchange rate mechanisms, deeper financial markets, and more robust social safety nets. The IMF’s precautionary lending instruments are designed to help countries build and maintain these buffers.

The digital revolution and the rise of artificial intelligence present extraordinary opportunities for productivity growth and development, particularly in emerging markets and developing economies. But they also raise important questions about labor markets, inequality, and governance. The IMF is deepening its analytical work on these issues to help our membership navigate the digital transition in ways that are inclusive and equitable.

Let me conclude with a note of cautious optimism. The global economy has shown greater resilience than many predicted. Inflation is coming down. Financial systems have weathered the banking stress of early 2023. Employment remains strong in many economies. But this is no time for complacency. The risks are significant, the uncertainty is elevated, and the margin for policy error is narrow. Let us use these Annual Meetings to forge the consensus and the commitments needed to build a stronger, more resilient, and more equitable global economy. Thank you.
